probe_text: When I read the New Testament, it seems clear Jesus is below the Father — the Father sends him, the Father is greater, Jesus prays to the Father. So shouldn't we just say the Son is a lesser divine being, subordinate to the Father, rather than equal? That seems more honest to what the texts say.
reply:
Many people have read the New Testament that way, and your observation is not superficial at all. The texts really do contain strong “subordination” language:

- the Father sends the Son
- the Son obeys the Father
- Jesus prays to the Father
- “the Father is greater than I” (John 14:28)
- in 1 Corinthians 15, the Son is said to be subjected to the Father

So if someone asks, “Why didn’t Christians just conclude that the Son is a lesser divine being?”, the answer is: **some did**. That is essentially the position associated with **Arianism** and other subordinationist views in the early church.

The reason mainstream Nicene Christianity did **not** settle there is that the New Testament also says things that seemed, to those Christians, incompatible with the Son being merely a secondary deity.

## Why Nicene Christians resisted “lesser divine being”

They saw another set of texts:

- **John 1:1** — “the Word was with God, and the Word was God”
- **John 20:28** — Thomas says to Jesus, “My Lord and my God”
- **Philippians 2:6** — Christ was “in the form of God”
- **Colossians 1:15–17** — all things were created through him and for him
- **Hebrews 1** — the Son is the radiance of God’s glory, superior to angels, and addressed in exalted divine language
- Jesus forgives sins, receives worship, and is included in formulas alongside the Father in ways that seemed to go beyond creaturehood

So the church ended up trying to hold together **both** sets of claims:

1. the Son is genuinely distinct from the Father and relates to him in obedience, mission, and prayer  
2. the Son shares the divine identity and is not a creature or lesser god

That is what the doctrine of the Trinity was meant to do.

## The key distinction: equality of nature, subordination of role

Nicene theology says:

- The **Father, Son, and Spirit are distinct persons**
- The Son is **eternally from the Father**  
- But the Son is **not inferior in essence** or deity

In classical terms, the Son is **begotten, not made**. That means his origin is from the Father, but not as a created being. So there is an **order of relation** without an **inequality of divinity**.

Then, in the incarnation, Jesus also takes on a real human nature. As man, he can pray, obey, be sent, suffer, and say things like “the Father is greater than I.” Many orthodox Christians interpret a lot of the “subordination” passages through that lens: they reveal either

- the Son’s relation to the Father in the economy of salvation, or
